name: Amenohabakiri
description: Amenohabakiri (天羽々斬) is a legendary sword in Japanese mythology, primarily known for its association with the god Susanoo-no-Mikoto. Its name translates roughly to 'Heavenly Feather Severing Sword' or 'Sword that Cuts Heavenly Plumes.' The sword is famed for its use in slaying the fearsome eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, Susanoo encountered an elderly couple lamenting the loss of their daughters to the Orochi, who demanded a maiden sacrifice annually. Susanoo, upon learning of this, offered to slay the beast in exchange for the hand of their last surviving daughter, Kushinadahime. He tricked the Orochi by preparing eight vats of strong sake, which the serpent consumed, rendering it completely drunk. While the Orochi was incapacitated, Susanoo drew Amenohabakiri and systematically severed each of its heads. During this process, the blade chipped upon striking something hard within the tail of the Orochi. Upon investigating, Susanoo discovered another legendary sword, the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (Grass-Cutting Sword), which he then presented to the goddess Amaterasu as a peace offering. Amenohabakiri is a symbol of courage, cunning, and the triumph over seemingly insurmountable odds. It represents the power to overcome evil and protect the innocent. The sword's association with Susanoo, a complex and often volatile deity, further adds to its mystique and significance within Shinto mythology. It is not only a weapon of great power but also a testament to the importance of strategy and resourcefulness in the face of adversity. The legacy of Amenohabakiri continues to resonate in Japanese culture, appearing in various forms of art, literature, and popular media, solidifying its place as a cornerstone of Japanese folklore.
subtitle: Heavenly Feather Severing Sword
origin: Forged by the gods or a divine swordsmith.

keyMyths.title: Slaying of Yamata no Orochi
keyMyths.description: Susanoo used Amenohabakiri to slay the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi after tricking it with sake. The sword proved crucial in defeating the monstrous beast and saving the maiden Kushinadahime.

keyMyths.title: Discovery of Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi
keyMyths.description: While slaying the Orochi, Amenohabakiri chipped on something hard within its tail, leading to the discovery of the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi. This sword was then presented to Amaterasu as a peace offering.

sources.author: Basil Hall Chamberlain
sources.description: Translation and commentary on the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation and commentary on the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.

sources.author: W.G. Aston

sources.description: Translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important source for Japanese mythology.
